10. Intractability

10.1. Definitions

We restrict the discussion to decision problems.

  • P: polynomial time to decide whether there is a solution.
  • NP: polynomial time to verify solution certificate of polynomial length
  • coNP: polynomial time to verify proof of nonexistence certificate of polynomial length. Just rephrase the decision problem as the converse of the decision problem (e.g. There is no solution to this sudoku).
  • NP is neither a subset of coNP nor the other way around. It seems more difficult to prove that a sudoku puzzle has no solution (coNP) than to find a solution (NP).
  • EXP: deterministic algo that runs in .
  • NP and coNP are subsets of EXP since they can try all possible certificates. P EXP by time hierarchy theorem.
  • It is believed that NP ≠ EXP and coNP ≠ EXP.
  • R is the set of decision problnems that can be solved in finite time. EXP R by the time hierarchy theorem.

Definition 4. Let and be decision problems. A polynomial time many-one reduction is a deterministic algorithm that takes in an instance of and outputs an instance of and runs in polynomial time such that = YES if and only if YES.

  • is at least as hard as A.

  • An NP problem is NP-hard if every problem in NP is polynomial time many-one reducible to it. It is NP-complete if it is NP hard and in NP.

    • An example of an NP hard but not NP complete problem is the optimization version of SAT, graph isomorphism, integer factorization, group isomorphism, unique-SAT

10.2. NP-complete problems

  • CIRCUIT-SAT: Given a polynomial circuit on inputs , does there exist an input so that evaluates to ?

    • NP-complete by Cook-Levin theorem

  • 3-SAT: Given a 3-CNF formula , is satisfiable (evaluates to )?

    • CNF means we have clasuses connected by ANDs, where each clause is an OR of exactly 3 literals, e.g. The input size is the number of clauses plus the number of variables.

  • VERTEX-COVER: Given a graph and integer , is there a set of at most vertices that form a vertex cover (i.e. for any , either or )?

    • To prove that VERTEX-COVER is NP-complete, it suffices to show that 3-SAT reduces to VERTEX-COVER. [TODO] Weird ass proof in L13/14.

  • SUBSET-SUM: Given a set of positive integers and an integer target , is there a subset whose elements sum to ?

    • Can solve using pseudopolynomial DP in
    • [TODO] We will show that VERTEX-COVER reduces to SUBSET-SUM.

    s_e_i edges give you flexibility: optimal solution may invovle using both vertices of an edge. Othertimes not.

  • BIN-ILP: Given a linear program (LP), is there a feasible solution to the LP where each variable ∈ {0, 1}?

    • Construction reduction from 3-SAT

  • CLIQUE: Given undirected and ? Is there a -clique?

    • Reduce VERTEX-COVER to CLIQUE
  • 3-color

10.3. Cook-Levin Theorem

We will show that CIRCUIT-SAT is NP complete. A given polynomial circuit can be evaluated in linear time using topological sort. Hence CIRCUIT SAT is NP. To show that it is NP hard, consider an instance of an NP problem . Then there exists a verifier that accepts a certificate whose number of bits is polynomial in . The reduction is to construct a circuit that implements . Since runs in polynomial time, the number of steps must be polynomial as well. Hence we can implement it step by step to get the circuit in polynomial time. It suffices to find a certificate that makes the circuit output . Hence this is an instance of CIRCUIT-SAT.

  • Suppose there is a solution to . Then by construction there is a solution to the CIRCUIT-SAT instance.
  • Suppose there is a solution to the CIRCUIT-SAT instance. Reconstruct as a certificate for and run it. Then and hence has a solution.

10.4. CIRCUIT-SAT reduces to 3-SAT

Next we will show that 3-SAT is NP complete via reduction from CIRCUIT-SAT. Let be the input variables of an instance of CIRCUIT-SAT, and let denote the outputs of gates, where is the output of the circuit and . Write where the clauses hardcode each binary gate:

For every one of those clauses, construct the truth table. For each of the false rows, add its negation as a clause. Then add an output clause to make sure the output is 1.

The gate is represented in 3CNF form as

The first one is to make sure we don’t get .

We’re basically using De Morgan’s rule to make sure the gates act correctly.
